cases.title: Tenant Lockout After Repair Dispute
strongest_arguments: California strongly disfavors landlord self-help; changing the locks while the tenant’s property remained inside is classic wrongful eviction exposure, and the landlord’s remedy was unlawful detainer, not lockout.

The record supports non-abandonment: furniture, medication, and work equipment remained in the unit at the time of the lock change, which is powerful objective evidence that possession had not been surrendered.

Habitability complaints were contemporaneous and documented in writing, with mold and heat outages reported and repairs deferred, supporting a Green/Knight rent-defense or at least an offset and undermining a simple unpaid-rent narrative.
vulnerabilities: The record does not quantify the rent abatement or prove that the defects justified full withholding, leaving exposure for some unpaid rent and breach damages.
There is no complete timeline showing whether the tenant had otherwise vacated, surrendered keys, or taken actions consistent with abandonment, so the landlord can still press that theory.
The defense lacks a full lease, rent ledger, and formal repair-and-deduct/notice record, which weakens the precision of the habitability and damages defenses.
evidence_gaps: Exact date and method of the lock change.
Whether any unlawful detainer notice or court filing occurred before the lockout.
Severity and duration of mold and heat defects.
Whether the tenant made partial payments or formally invoked any statutory repair remedy.
Lease language governing default, abandonment, and landlord entry.
Any proof the tenant surrendered keys, changed mailing address, or moved most belongings before lockout.
